law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Obligation to define a specified purpose for personal data processing
description: Personal data must be processed only for specified purposes and cannot be further used incompatibly
full_text: Every controller shall ensure that personal data is processed for a a) specified; purposes and such personal data shall not be further processed in a manner which is incompatible with such purposes.
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Your personal data can only be used for a clearly defined purpose. If someone uses your data for something you did not agree to, this is a violation of your rights.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Obligation to define legitimate purposes for personal data processing
description: Further processing of personal data for permitted research, statistical, or archiving purposes is allowed
full_text: Subject to the provisions of section 10 of this Act, further processing of such personal data by a controller for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific research, historical research or statistical purposes shall not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c) of subsection (1)
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Even when data is used for research or archiving beyond the original purpose, strict safeguards must apply. This exception does not permit misuse of your data.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Obligation to confine personal data processing to the defined purpose
description: Personal data must be adequate for the purpose of collection/processing
full_text: Every controller shall ensure that personal data that is processed shall be a) adequate to the extent as is necessary in relation to the purpose for which such data shall be collected or processed.
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Only the minimum data necessary for the task may be collected. Collecting more of your information than required is a violation.

penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Your personal data must be accurate. If it is wrong or outdated, the data controller must correct or delete it promptly.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Obligation to limit the period of retention
description: Personal data must only be retained for as long as necessary, unless used for permitted research or archiving purposes
full_text: Every controller shall ensure that personal data that is being processed shall be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects only for such period as may be necessary or required for the purposes for which such personal data is processed: Provided however, subject to the provisions of section 10 of this Act, a controller may store personal data for longer periods in so far as the personal data shall be processed further for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific research, historical research or statistical purposes.
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Your personal data should not be kept longer than necessary. Once the purpose is fulfilled, it must be deleted or anonymized.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Obligation to maintain Integrity and confidentiality
description: Personal data must be protected against unauthorized or unlawful processing
full_text: Every controller shall ensure integrity and confidentiality of personal data that is being processed, by using appropriate technical and organizational measures including encryption, pseudonymisation, anonymisation or access controls or such other measures as may be prescribed so as to prevent the a) unauthorized or unlawful processing of personal data;
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Your data must be protected from unauthorized or unlawful processing using technical safeguards such as encryption and access controls.

title: Obligation to provide information referred to in Schedule V

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Accountability in the processing of personal data
description: Controllers must maintain catalogued records to demonstrate compliance with PDPA obligations
full_text: It shall be the duty of every controller to implement internal controls and procedures, (hereinafter referred to as the 'Data Protection Management Programme') that a) establishes and maintains duly catalogued records to demonstrate the manner in which the implementation of the data protection obligations referred to in sections 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11 are carried out by the controller; for the purpose of complying with the obligations referred to in sections 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11.
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Organizations must maintain documented records proving how they comply with data protection rules — you can request evidence of compliance.

title: Accountability in the processing of personal data
description: Controllers must design the programme based on processing scale and sensitivity
full_text: It shall be the duty of every controller to implement internal controls and procedures, (hereinafter referred to as the 'Data Protection Management Programme') that b) is designed on the basis of structure, scale, volume and sensitivity of processing activities of the controller; for the purpose of complying with the obligations referred to in sections 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11.
penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: Data protection programs must be designed to match the scale and sensitivity of processing activities — larger or more sensitive operations require stronger protections.

penalty: May result in imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fines up to $50,000. Subject to enforcement by Data Protection Authority.

title: Securing unauthorized access to information with intent to commit an offence.
description: Unauthorized access to computer-held information with intent to commit an offence constitutes an offence
full_text: Any person who intentionally does any act, in order to secure for himself or for any other person, access to b) any information held in any computer knowing or having reason to believe that he has no lawful authority to secure such access and with the intention of committing an offence under this Act or any other law for the time being in force, shall be guilty of an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Unauthorized access to information with criminal intent is punishable by higher fine or imprisonment or both
explanation: Accessing computer-held information without authority and with criminal intent is a serious offence under Sri Lankan law.

title: Causing a computer to perform a function without lawful authority an offence.
description: Intentionally causing a computer to perform unauthorized functions leading to modification or damage constitutes an offence
full_text: Any person who, intentionally and without lawful authority causes a computer to perform any function knowing or having reason to believe that such function will result in unauthorized modification or damage or potential damage to any computer or computer system or computer programme shall be guilty of an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Unauthorized modification or damage to computer systems is punishable by fine or imprisonment or both
explanation: Deliberately causing a computer to perform unauthorized functions — such as altering, deleting, or damaging data — is a criminal offence.

description: Buying, receiving, retaining, selling, or dealing with unlawfully obtained computer information constitutes an offence
full_text: Any person who, knowing or having reason to believe that any other person has without lawful authority obtained information from a computer or a storage medium of a computer, a) buys, receives, retains, sells, or in any manner deals with; any such information shall be guilty of an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not less than one hundred thousand rupees and not exceeding three hundred thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description for a term not less than six months and not exceeding three years, or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Dealing with unlawfully obtained computer information is punishable by fine or imprisonment or both
explanation: Buying, selling, or dealing with information that was unlawfully obtained from a computer is a criminal offence, even if you did not hack the system yourself.

penalty: Offering to deal with unlawfully obtained computer information is punishable by fine or imprisonment or both
explanation: Offering to sell or trade unlawfully obtained computer information is a crime, even if no actual transaction takes place.

penalty: Acquiring unlawfully obtained computer information is punishable by fine or imprisonment or both

description: Disclosure of service access information without authority or in breach of trust constitutes an offence

penalty: Unauthorized disclosure of access-enabling information is punishable by fine, imprisonment, or both
explanation: Disclosing information that gives access to a service — such as passwords or login credentials — without authorization is a crime.

full_text: Any person, whether in or outside Sri Lanka who, by means of an online account, cheats by a) pretending to be some other person; commits the offence of 'online cheating by personation' and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years or to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand rupees, or to both such imprisonment and fine.
penalty: Online personation is punishable by imprisonment, fine, or both
explanation: Pretending to be someone else online to deceive or cheat is a specific criminal offence under Sri Lankan law. This includes impersonation on social media platforms.

title: Communicating statements to cause harassment & c.
description: Publishing private information to cause harassment constitutes an offence under OSA
full_text: Any person, whether in or outside Sri Lanka who willfully makes or communicates a statement, with intention to cause harassment to another person (in this section referred to as the 'target person'), by publishing any private information of the target person or a related person of the target person, and as a result causes the target person or any other person harassment, commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years or to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand rupees and in the event of a second or subsequent conviction, such term of imprisonment or fine or both such imprisonment and fine may be doubled.
penalty: Publishing private information to cause harassment is punishable by imprisonment, fine, or both; penalties double for repeat offences
explanation: Publishing private information about someone online with the intent to harass, cause distress, or damage their reputation is an offence under the Online Safety Act.

law_name: Right to Information Act, No. 12 of 2016
title: When right of access may be denied.
description: Information requests can be refused if disclosure of personal information invades privacy without public interest or consent
full_text: Subject to the provisions of subsection (2) a request under this Act for access to information shall be refused, where a) the information relates to personal information the disclosure of which has no relationship to any public activity or interest, or which would cause unwarranted invasion of the privacy of the individual unless the larger public interest justifies the disclosure of such information or the person concerned has consented in writing to such disclosure;
penalty: Disclosure of personal information without public interest or consent is not permitted under RTI
explanation: A public authority can refuse to release your personal information if doing so would invade your privacy without a valid public interest justification or your consent.

title: Requirements for original form
description: Electronic documents or records can satisfy original-form requirements if integrity is assured and information is available for reference
full_text: Where the law requires information to be presented or retained in its original form, that requirement shall be deemed to be satisfied by a data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication in electronic form if there exists a reliable assurance as to the integrity of the information from the time when it was first generated in its final form as a data message, electronic document, electronic record or communication or otherwise and the information contained in the data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication is available and can be used for subsequent reference.
penalty: Original-form requirements satisfied through electronic documents or records
explanation: Electronic documents are legally valid as originals if their integrity is maintained and they are accessible for future reference.

penalty: Retention in electronic form satisfies legal requirements for identification purposes

title: Legal recognition of electronic signatures
description: Electronic signatures recognized if signer identified and intention indicated
full_text: Where any written law provides that a communication or any other document shall,be signed or bear the signature of any person, then notwithstanding anything to the contrary in such written law, that requirement shall be deemed to be met in relation to a data message, electronic document, electronic record or communication if (a)a method is used to identify that person and to indicate that person’s intention in respect of the information contained in the electronic communication.
penalty: Electronic signatures satisfy legal signature requirements
explanation: An electronic signature is legally recognized if it clearly identifies the signer and indicates their intention to sign.

title: Legal recognition of electronic signatures
description: Signature methods must be reliable for the communication purpose
full_text: Where any written law provides that a communication or any other document shall,be signed or bear the signature of any person, then notwithstanding anything to the contrary in such written law, that requirement shall be deemed to be met in relation to a data message, electronic document, electronic record or communication if (b)The method used is (i)as reliable as appropriate for the purpose for which the electronic communication was generated, sent or communicated, in the light of all the circumstances, including any relevant agreement.
penalty: May result in civil penalties up to $25,000 and enforcement action by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: The method used for an electronic signature must be reliably suited to the purpose of the communication it is attached to.

title: Attribution of electronic records
description: Electronic record attributed to originator if sent by originator
full_text: Unless otherwise agreed as between an originator and the addressee, a data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication shall be deemed to be that of the originator, if it was sent (a)by the originator himself.
penalty: Originator is responsible for records sent by themselves
explanation: An electronic record is legally attributed to the person who originally sent it.

title: Attribution of electronic records
description: Electronic record is attributed to the originator if sent by an authorized person on their behalf
full_text: Unless otherwise agreed as between an originator and the addressee, a data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication shall be deemed to be that of the originator, if it was sent (b) by a person who had the authority to act for and on behalf of the originator in respect of that data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication.
penalty: Authorized persons can act on behalf of the originator for electronic records
explanation: An electronic record sent by an authorized representative on behalf of someone else is legally attributed to that person.

title: Attribution of electronic records
description: Addressee may act assuming originator if authenticity not doubted
full_text: Unless otherwise agreed as between the originator and the addressee, the addressee is to regard a data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication as being that of the originator, and is entitled to act on that assumption, if (a)the addressee has no reason to doubt the authenticity of the data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication.
penalty: Addressee can rely on the record being from originator
explanation: The recipient of an electronic record may reasonably rely on it as genuine, unless they have cause to doubt its authenticity.

title: Attribution of electronic records
description: Addressee may rely unless reasonable doubt exists
full_text: Unless otherwise agreed as between the originator and the addressee, the addressee is to regard a data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication as being that of the originator, and is entitled to act on that assumption, if (b)there do not exist any circumstances where the addressee knows, or ought to have known by exercising reasonable care, that the data message, electronic document, electronic record or other communication was authentic.
penalty: May result in civil penalties up to $25,000 and enforcement action by Data Protection Authority.
explanation: A recipient may act on an electronic record unless reasonable grounds exist to doubt that it came from the stated originator.

title: Deceiving or misleading persons using a telecommunication system
description: Misrepresentation using telecom systems is an offence
full_text: Every person who, with the intention of misrepresenting, deceiving or misleading any other person omits, changes or modifies the calling party number or introduces any other calling party number other than his own number commits an offence and shall on conviction by a Magistrate be liable to a fine not exceeding ten million rupees and to imprisonment of either description for a term not less than one year and not exceeding three years or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Fine up to LKR 10000000 and 1–3 years imprisonment
explanation: Using a telecommunications system to deceive or mislead another person is a criminal offence under Sri Lankan law.

title: Providing false information to obtain telecommunication service
description: Providing false identity to obtain telecom service is an offence
full_text: Every person who, willfully provides false information as to the identity of user to obtain telecommunication service commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not less than one hundred thousand rupees and not exceeding one million rupees and to imprisonment of either description for a term not less than one year and not exceeding three years or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Fine LKR 100000–1000000 and 1–3 years imprisonment
explanation: Providing false identity information to obtain a telecom service — such as a SIM card registered under someone else's name — is a criminal offence.

title: Offences by telecommunication officer &c.
description: Unauthorized disclosure of message contents is an offence
full_text: Every telecommunication officer, or any person having official duties in connection with a telecommunication service, who (c) Otherwise than in pursuance of his official duty or as directed by court discloses the contents or any part of the contents of any message to any person other than the person to whom the message is addressed, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding ten thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding six months or to both such fine and imprisonment.
penalty: Fine up to LKR 10000 or up to 6 months imprisonment
explanation: A telecommunications officer who discloses the contents of your messages without authorization is committing a criminal offence.

title: Penalty for intrusion into the contents of a message &c.
description: Unauthorized intrusion into message contents or usage info is an offence
full_text: Every person who without lawful authority intrudes (a)into the contents of a message or its usage information by electronic or other means shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not less than twenty thousand rupees and not exceeding one hundred thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description to a term not exceeding two years or to both such fine and such imprisonment.
penalty: Fine LKR 200000–100000 or up to 2 years imprisonment
explanation: Unauthorized access to the contents or usage information of someone's telecommunications is a crime.

title: Wilful interception of telecommunication transmission.
description: Wilful interception not intended for general reception is an offence
full_text: Every person who wilfully seeks to intercept and improperly acquaint himself with the contents of any telecommunication transmission not intended for general reception shall be guilty of an offence, and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding ten thousand rupees or to imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding six months or to both such fine and such imprisonment.
penalty: Fine up to LKR 10000 or up to 6 months imprisonment
explanation: Deliberately intercepting a private telecommunications transmission not intended for you is a criminal offence.

title: Circulating false report with intent to cause mutiny or an offence against the State
description: Communicating false statements intending to cause mutiny, public alarm, or induce others to commit offences
full_text: Any person, whether in or outside Sri Lanka who communicates any false statement, with intent to cause any officer, sailor, soldier, or airman in the navy, army or air force of Sri Lanka to mutiny, or with intent to cause fear or alarm to the public, induces any other person to commit an offence against the State or against the public tranquillity, commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding seven years, or to a fine not exceeding seven hundred thousand rupees, or to both such imprisonment and fine.
penalty: Imprisonment up to 7 years, fine up to LKR 700000, or both
explanation: Making or sharing false statements intended to cause mutiny, public alarm, or to induce others to commit offences is a criminal offence under the Online Safety Act.

title: Disturbing a religious assembly by a false statement
description: Communicating false statements that disturb religious assemblies or worship
full_text: Any person, whether in or outside Sri Lanka who by communicating a false statement, voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies, commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding three years, or to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand rupees, or to both such imprisonment and fine.
penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years, fine up to LKR 300000, or both
explanation: Publishing or communicating false statements that disturb lawful religious worship or ceremonies is an offence; this can include using a person's name or username online to cause distress or fear.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Right to rectification or completion
description: Data subjects have the right to request controllers to rectify or complete inaccurate or incomplete personal data
full_text: Every data subject shall have the right to request a controller in writing to rectify or complete the personal data relating to such data subject which is either inaccurate or incomplete, and the controller shall, upon such a written request made by the data subject, rectify or complete the personal data without undue delay subject to the provisions of section 17: Provided however, the provisions of this section shall not impose any obligation on a controller to collect and process any additional personal data that is not required for the purpose of processing: Provided further, where a controller is required to maintain personal data for the evidentiary purposes under any written law or on an order of a competent court, the controller shall refrain from further processing such personal data without rectifying.
penalty: Failure to rectify or complete personal data upon valid request may constitute a PDPA violation
explanation: You can request a controller to correct or complete inaccurate or incomplete personal data and the controller must do so without undue delay, subject to limited exceptions.

law_name: Personal Data Protection Act, No. 9 of 2022
title: Personal data breach notifications
description: Controllers must notify the Authority in the event of a personal data breach
full_text: In the event of a personal data breach, a controller shall notify the Authority, regarding such personal data breach in such form, manner and within such period of time as may be determined by rules made under this Act.
penalty: Failure to notify the Authority of a personal data breach may result in enforcement action under the PDPA
explanation: If personal data is breached, controllers are required to notify the regulator (the Authority) in the manner and timeframes set by the PDPA rules.
